## [00:00 - 03:02]

Number 16, purchase intent. Now this is a potentially big one. A big factor impacting your success kind of depending upon your industry. If you're e-commerce, this can be very impactful. So purchase intent, if you're not familiar, it's evidence that someone wants to buy something. It's not just that they show like they have interest in something related to your product. It's they've actually performed an action that shows that they're wanting to buy something from you. So they performed a search looking for a solution. They visited your product page. They subscribed to something further up the funnel related to your product. And some campaigns, the success of them really require purchase intent. So in some cases, products and services are only desired when they're needed. So for example, you don't need a plumber when your pipes are fine. So ideally, you want to be able to reach people who have already shown that they're in need of a plumber as opposed to just blanketing your entire city saying, hey, we're a plumber when you need us, which awareness can be good. Don't get me wrong, but the success rate of individual ads won't be great in that case. You're just going for an awareness play. So if we can reach people who have shown that they want to buy something that we can offer, that's powerful. So remarketing your website visits should be step one. We've already talked about this and how important that is and the value of it. That's important. We've got to do it. So let's reach those who recently went to your website. If your traffic is low. That said, it's difficult, very difficult with Facebook ads. And this is where I see a lot of companies struggle, a lot of advertisers struggle when they don't have that remarketing capability, and they don't know where to go. Problem is, even though Facebook now has a search placement, it's not based off of keywords. So unlike Google, where you could buy search terms where people are looking for your solution, I'll go back to the plumber example. How do you reach people who are looking for a plumber if you don't get website visitors? It's almost impossible. So without those search, true search terms, but what you can do is you can run Google ads on search terms, while also running Facebook ads. So do them both if this is your issue. Okay, if you're not, if you don't have a lot of website traffic and you rely on Facebook ads, and you rely heavily on purchase intent, run Google ads on certain search terms

to attract people who are looking for your solution, and then remarket to those people who visited your website as a result of those ads. So they can be used in concert.
